Maintaining compliance reduces legal risk, improves customer trust, and strengthens organizational security. Regular audits are performed to verify ongoing compliance.

terms.title: Merkle Tree
terms.definition: A Merkle Tree is a hierarchical cryptographic data structure in which every non-leaf node contains the hash of its child nodes, allowing efficient integrity verification of large datasets.
examPoints: Individual data blocks are first hashed, and those hashes are repeatedly combined and hashed until a single Merkle Root is produced. Changing any data block changes the Merkle Root, making tampering immediately detectable. Merkle Trees are fundamental to Bitcoin, Ethereum, Git, distributed storage systems, and blockchain technology. They enable efficient verification without processing the entire dataset.

terms.title: Hash Chain
terms.definition: A Hash Chain is a sequence of hashes where each hash depends on the previous one, creating a cryptographically linked chain of data.
examPoints: Each block or record contains the hash of the previous block, making any modification immediately detectable because it changes all subsequent hashes. Hash Chains provide integrity, immutability, and tamper evidence. They are widely used in blockchain systems, secure logging, timestamping, and audit trails. Breaking a Hash Chain requires recomputing every subsequent hash.

terms.title: Consensus
terms.definition: Consensus is the process by which distributed blockchain participants agree on the validity and order of transactions without relying on a central authority.
examPoints: Consensus algorithms ensure that every node maintains the same version of the blockchain ledger. Common mechanisms include Proof of Work (PoW), Proof of Stake (PoS), Practical Byzantine Fault Tolerance (PBFT), and Delegated Proof of Stake (DPoS). Consensus prevents double spending and malicious manipulation of blockchain data. It is fundamental to decentralized systems.

terms.title: Wallet Keys
terms.definition: Wallet Keys are cryptographic public-private key pairs that enable users to securely own, transfer, and digitally sign cryptocurrency transactions.
examPoints: The private key authorizes spending and must remain secret, while the public key is used to derive wallet addresses and verify digital signatures. Losing the private key usually means permanent loss of access to cryptocurrency assets. Wallet software securely stores these keys using encryption or hardware security devices. Blockchain ownership depends entirely on possession of the private key.

terms.title: Digital Signatures
terms.definition: A Digital Signature is a cryptographic mechanism that uses a private key to sign digital data and a corresponding public key to verify the authenticity and integrity of that data.
examPoints: Digital signatures provide authentication, integrity, and non-repudiation. The sender hashes the message and encrypts the hash using their private key. The receiver decrypts the signature using the sender's public key and compares the resulting hash with a newly computed hash of the received message. Digital signatures are widely used in PKI, HTTPS, code signing, email security, and blockchain transactions.

terms.title: CrypTool
terms.definition: CrypTool is an educational cryptography software suite used to demonstrate and experiment with cryptographic algorithms, protocols, and security concepts.
examPoints: It provides graphical implementations of classical ciphers, modern encryption algorithms, hashing, digital signatures, PKI, RSA, AES, ECC, and cryptanalysis techniques. CrypTool helps students visualize how cryptographic algorithms work internally. It is widely used in cybersecurity education, research, and laboratory exercises. Multiple versions, including CrypTool 2, support advanced security analysis.

terms.title: Browser Trust
terms.definition: Browser Trust is the mechanism by which web browsers determine whether a website's digital certificate should be trusted during an HTTPS connection.
examPoints: Browsers verify the certificate chain, issuer signature, validity period, hostname (SAN), revocation status, and whether the Root CA exists in the browser or operating system's trust store. If validation succeeds, the browser establishes a secure TLS session and displays the HTTPS padlock. Invalid or untrusted certificates trigger security warnings. Browser trust is the foundation of secure web communication.

terms.title: DNS Configuration
terms.definition: DNS Configuration is the process of configuring Domain Name System records so that domain names correctly resolve to the intended IP addresses and network services.
examPoints: Common DNS records include A, AAAA, CNAME, MX, TXT, and NS records. Proper DNS configuration is essential for HTTPS certificate validation, email delivery, web hosting, and many Internet services. Certificate Authorities often verify domain ownership using DNS records during certificate issuance. Incorrect DNS configuration can prevent websites and security services from functioning correctly.

terms.title: Email Signing
terms.definition: Email Signing is the process of digitally signing email messages using a private key to verify the sender's identity and protect message integrity.
examPoints: The sender creates a digital signature using their private key, and recipients verify it using the sender's public certificate. Email signing confirms that the message has not been modified during transmission and proves the sender's identity. Technologies such as S/MIME and PGP implement digital email signatures. Email signing provides authentication, integrity, and non-repudiation.

terms.title: Email Encryption
terms.definition: Email Encryption is the process of encrypting email messages so that only the intended recipient can read their contents.
examPoints: The sender encrypts the message using the recipient's public key, and the recipient decrypts it using their private key. This ensures confidentiality even if the email is intercepted during transmission. S/MIME and PGP are the most widely used email encryption standards. Encrypted email protects sensitive business, financial, healthcare, and government communications from unauthorized access.
